lesson: MFA requires a second device or token, meaning even if a user falls for a phishing email and gives away their password, the attacker still cannot log in.
Ransomware encrypts your files so you cannot use them. Immutable, offline backups allow you to wipe the infected systems and restore your data without paying the ransom.
Software vulnerabilities are open doors for attackers. Applying vendor security patches promptly closes these doors before automated scanners can find them.
A DDoS attack attempts to crash a server by flooding it with fake traffic. A WAF filters out malicious traffic while letting real users through.
Attackers can hide malicious commands in AI prompts to bypass safety filters. Sanitizing inputs ensures the AI treats user text strictly as data, not as executable instructions.
AI can perfectly clone voices and faces. To combat this, organizations must rely on cryptographic identity verification (like digital signatures) rather than just visual or audio confirmation.
Attackers can alter the data an AI learns from to introduce biases or backdoors. Data provenance tracking ensures every piece of training data comes from a verified, uncompromised source.

SQL injection tricks a database into running attacker-supplied commands by embedding them in user input. Parameterized queries separate code from data, so user input is never interpreted as a SQL command.

A man-in-the-middle attack intercepts communications between two parties. TLS (the protocol behind HTTPS) encrypts all traffic and uses certificates to verify server identity, making interception both detectable and unreadable.

Insiders — employees or contractors — can abuse their access to steal or sabotage data. Zero Trust assumes no user or device is trusted by default. Every access request is verified, logged, and scoped to the minimum privilege required for the task.

Supply chain attacks compromise a trusted vendor or open-source package to distribute malware to thousands of downstream targets. An SBOM — a full inventory of every software component and its origin — lets organizations quickly identify and patch affected systems when a component is found to be malicious.

Password spraying tries a few common passwords across thousands of accounts to avoid lockout triggers. PAM enforces strong, unique credentials for privileged accounts, applies just-in-time access, and logs every session — drastically shrinking the attack surface even when one password is guessed.

Attackers can extract a proprietary AI model by querying it thousands of times and training a clone on the responses. Model watermarking embeds imperceptible signatures into outputs so that stolen models can be identified and traced back to the theft.

Employees using unauthorized AI tools to process company data create invisible data leakage risks — sensitive information may be used to train third-party models. An AI governance policy defines approved tools, data classification rules, and acceptable use so organizations maintain control over their data.
